Alright, internal surveys. What are internal surveys? There are surveys that provide open-ended questions so that the team members can answer you openly, honestly, and give you feedback so you can identify areas of opportunity more simply. Alright, so something I want to add here as a disclaimer is that if you have a team of less than five and you are the point of contact for all of them, this is probably not something you need to do yet. I would say once you range from five to ten to start sending this out every month or every quarter and then you increase it as the business grows. Okay, so increase it means if you're only sending it out quarterly, you start sending it out monthly once you get above, you know, maybe ten teammates. If you start communicating with people less and you're out of the gym more, you want this more frequently. Okay, so it varies and you just want to test it, try it, see what works for your business. Right? Why is this so important? Not only does it tell you as the owner and the leaders, right, so anyone in management how engaged each employee is based on their answers or if they neglect to even fill it out, if they always neglect filling it out, if they give crappy short answers, right? But it also shows who would like to grow, who needs help, and who gives those who want to take on more opportunity to raise their hand. So if somebody does want to take on more, then they can raise their hand and say, I would like to, right? And those are typically the people that are the most engaged, right? So how do you do this? We use Wufu and so we just send out a link in an email. How often, one time per month, like I said, lower frequency if you feel like you have a smaller team, higher if you are getting yourself out of the gym and growing your team. How do you do this? You send an email with the link embedded and then instructions on the team to fill it out and typically something that you could do is you could leave them 10 minutes of the monthly meeting. So you could say at the end of the month, and you would be like, hey guys, we're all going to stay here. I want everyone to fill out the survey. You're not going to share your answers, but I just want to give you the time to do it right now. Pretty simple, right? Here's an example of an email that we also have in the download section. Hey team, I've initiated sending out a monthly internal survey to the team. This is reviewed by solely myself. So I would like you to fill it out as honestly and thoroughly as possible. The better your answers, the better I can see where we need to improve. As what each of you see from your position is much different than what I see and the more perspective I can get from y'all, the better I can help. Please complete by end of day Friday. Here's the link to the survey. This is actually the email that I sent to our team when we first started doing these two years ago. Here's the example questions. These are the two that I always ask and I actually have very high engagement and get a lot of response from. One, what are the biggest obstacles or constraints you're currently facing? And then two, where do you see the biggest opportunities for yourself and the company to improve? Okay, so obstacles and constraints, they might be things you don't even know about. And so it's really helpful when your team starts telling you like, well, this is really, and you're like, wow, I could easily fix that for you. But if I had not known, I would have been able to fix it, right? And then biggest opportunities for yourself and the company to improve. Well, if it's, you know, five of your trainers are telling you that they feel like the sessions aren't long enough or too long or could be better or the programming needs to be updated, then suddenly something that you weren't even thinking about, because you're probably thinking about getting people in the door and revenue is something that you can give them the opportunity to solve. So a lot of the times you collect the data from these, and then you can review it on your weekly meeting. And you can say, here's where I see opportunities for improvement. And I would like somebody to own these, right? And so it shouldn't be you as the owner owning all of the solutions to the problems, right?
